topic: Aspects of Political Life in the Hispanic World
subject: spanish

items.q: When do you use the imperfect rather than the preterite?
items.correct: For ongoing, habitual, or descriptive past actions.
items.misconception: When the action happened a long time ago.
items.random_wrong: When you're unsure of the exact date.
items.why: Students choose tense by recency. The distinction is ASPECTUAL: preterite = completed/single ('comí' I ate); imperfect = ongoing/habitual ('comía' I used to eat / I was eating).

items.q: How do you say 'I love films' in Spanish?
items.why: Students translate 'amar' (which is for people/love) or forget gustar/encantar structure. ENCANTAR works like gustar - backwards. Subject = películas (plural), so verb = ENCANTAN. 'Me encanta el cine' (singular subject) vs 'me encantan las películas' (plural).
Students apply English pronunciation. Spanish H is ALWAYS silent. 'Hola' is 'OH-lah', not 'HO-lah'. (The breathy sound students associate with H comes from j or g+e/i in Spanish.)
It's Me gusta el español.. “Me gusto el español.” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which sentence means 'Maths is more difficult than English'?
items.correct: Las matemáticas son más difíciles que el inglés.
items.misconception: Las matemáticas es más difícil que el inglés.
items.random_wrong: Las matemáticas son menos difíciles que el inglés.
items.why: It's Las matemáticas son más difíciles que el inglés.. “Las matemáticas es más difícil que el inglés.” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many subject pronouns does Spanish have (yo, tú, él/ella/usted, nosotros, vosotros, ellos/ellas/ustedes)?
items.why: Exact answer: 6 pronouns. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
